behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module handoff: raw-body capture + HMAC verifier + enqueue gate. A unit test of only the verifier misses bugs where middleware/body normalization changes bytes before verification (module-coordination-probe). System was considered but rejected because real network/OS behavior is not required; in-process HTTP wiring is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler with real request parsing middleware in-process, send a request with intentionally mismatched `X-Signature-256` over the exact raw payload bytes, assert HTTP 401, and assert queue `write/enqueue` was not called and no persisted record exists.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is ordering across handler and persistence module. Unit tests on handler alone can miss commit/ack ordering bugs. System was considered and rejected: no real external network/subprocess is required; local persistence wiring catches the real failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use real queue persistence module (local DB/filesystem-backed test instance). Instrument response completion time and queue commit time; assert commit completes before response is sent (200 only after durable write).

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Behavior validates decoupling between enqueue path and async consumer path (cross-module orchestration). A unit test of one function may miss accidental `await` on processor promise. System rejected because real OS/network is not necessary to expose this await-coupling bug.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + real queue adapter + a downstream processor stub that blocks (e.g., 10s). Send valid event and assert 200 is returned quickly while processor is still running, then confirm processing completes later.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Real wall-clock latency under concurrency depends on event-loop scheduling, socket handling, and runtime contention. In-process mocks can hide true timing/backpressure behavior (REAL-INFRA + MOCK-HIDE). Higher levels (Agentic/Workflow) are disqualified because no AI-model decisions are involved.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service process, generate concurrent webhook traffic while earlier events trigger intentionally slow downstream work, and measure end-to-end HTTP latency at the client. Assert each response is <=3s under the defined concurrent load profile.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at handler-to-persistent-queue state boundary (ID check + durable state). Unit logic tests can miss race/constraint behavior across modules. System rejected because no real external network/subprocess is necessary; local persistence integration is enough.
behaviors.test_description: Using real persistence-backed queue in test mode, submit the same event ID twice (including near-concurrent submits). Assert exactly one durable queue record exists and second request is treated as duplicate per contract.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This checks contract mapping across modules: queue-capacity error -> HTTP status/header. Unit branch tests can miss mismatch in real error object/type propagation. System rejected because behavior text does not require real OS/network effects, only module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue module configured to signal `full` state. Send valid webhook and assert response is 503 and includes `Retry-After` with expected format/value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Core failure boundary is inside persistence/queue modules (capacity accounting + durable write decision). A mocked queue would always pass and hide real enforcement bugs. System considered but rejected because this can be caught with local persistence integration (no required external subprocess/network).
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate queue with small fixed capacity in a real local persistence backend, fill to limit, then attempt one more enqueue. Assert overflow is rejected, stored count remains at capacity, and restart/reload still reflects the same capped state.
